title: Jewellery soak cleaner
subtitle: A warm washing-up liquid soak for cleaning gold, silver, and hard gemstone jewellery
excerpt: Most everyday jewellery tarnish and grime responds to a 10-minute soak in warm water with a drop of washing-up liquid and a gentle scrub with a soft toothbrush. This method is safe for solid gold, sterling silver, and hard gemstones such as diamonds, sapphires, and rubies. It is not suitable for pearls, opals, emeralds, or any porous or resin-set stones.
sourceNotes: Jewellery cleaning with mild detergent and warm water: standard gemological care reference (GIA, gemological institute guidance on home jewellery care). Safe gemstones for water soak: Mohs hardness 7+ considered safe for brief water immersion; opals, emeralds, and pearls are porous or fragile and should not be soaked. Castile soap or washing-up liquid as a degreaser for metal jewellery: non-ionic surfactants leave no residue when rinsed.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make fresh each time; do not store.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Use immediately and discard. Do not store this solution.

glossaryTerms.definition: A surface-active agent that reduces the surface tension of water, allowing it to lift grease and grime from surfaces. Washing-up liquid is a surfactant.

content.text: Most jewellery that has lost its shine is simply dirty: a film of skin oil, lotion, and grime has built up on the metal and around the settings. A soak in warm water with a drop of washing-up liquid and a light scrub with a soft toothbrush removes this without abrasion or harsh chemicals. This method works on solid gold (all carats), sterling silver, and hard gemstones with a Mohs hardness of 7 or above, including diamonds, sapphires, rubies, and topazes. Do not use this method on pearls, opals, emeralds, coral, amber, jet, or any piece with a glued setting or a resin fill.

attrs.title: Stones that should not be soaked
attrs.body: Pearls, opals, emeralds, turquoise, malachite, amber, coral, and jet are porous or fragile. Soaking them in water, even briefly, can cause surface damage, cloudiness, or loosening of settings. Wipe these pieces with a barely damp cloth instead.

items.prepNote: warm, not boiling; around 40 degrees C
or a drop of castile soap; any mild, fragrance-free washing-up liquid

content.text: Add the washing-up liquid to the warm water and stir briefly. Place the jewellery in the bowl and leave to soak for 10 minutes. The
in the liquid loosens the oil film during the soak so the scrub step requires minimal pressure.

Remove each piece from the bowl and scrub gently with a soft toothbrush, paying attention to the underside of stones and any engraved areas where grime collects. Rinse each piece thoroughly under warm running water, holding it over a cupped hand or a bowl rather than directly over a drain. Pat dry with a lint-free cloth and leave on a clean surface to dry completely before storing.

Silver that has darkened significantly with tarnish (silver sulphide, the grey-black layer) will not respond to this method alone. A bicarbonate of soda paste or an electrolytic tarnish removal method is more effective for heavy silver tarnish. This soak is for maintenance cleaning of pieces in regular use, not for restoring heavily tarnished pieces.
